title: 2026/04/02 - JU [Part 4]
displayTitle: Justice Committee - Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026 [Part 4]
topic: Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026
description: Thursday, 2 April 2026 | Justice Committee Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026 Hearing of Evidence Department of Corrections Jeremy Lightfoot, Chief Executive Juanita Ryan, DCE Pae Ora Tom Baker, Chief Digital Officer English Language Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Pirirakau tribal Authority - Incorporated Heta Gardiner, Kaihautū/General Manager Graeme Edgeler Rimu Bhooi Dean Knight Jette de Jong Tatyana Protsenko Te Auta Sam-Turner
descriptionSummary: Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026. Department of Corrections. Jeremy Lightfoot, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Yes. Yeah. But anyway, congratulations for that. Hey, now, we've got a bit of time with you this morning. Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. As a committee, as you know, we love asking questions. I have become accustomed to that reality, yeah, Chair.

Are you comfortable if we just make some- Mm... for this occasion, very short remarks, just to try and frame- Yeah... the conversation and shape some of the questions. So thanks, firstly, for inviting us to talk to you about the long-term insight briefing. Uh-huh. And I think you also wanted to maybe spend a short amount of time on the mandamus order. Yes. So- We've got one member particularly interested.

So I just- Everyone should be interested. Draw your attention. If you need to talk about the legal situation- Yep... so be upfront. Awesome. Yeah, so I'll cover that in my opening remarks just in terms of framing it, because I am able to talk about it to a level, and then there are some limitations. But can I just make sure I've introduced my colleagues who are sitting at the table? You are familiar, I think-... we've got Juanita Ryan, deputy chief executive at the moment, Communities, Partnerships, and Pathways. And Tom Baker, who's our chief

digital officer. So they're both here. I assume the chief digital officer's role here is not needing to be explained. And Juanita obviously brings lots of insight around the rehabilitative reintegrative services, where we think there's a good opportunity, and also at the moment is covering the community partnerships and pathways space where also technology has a quite significant role to play.

Corrections' LTIB on potential technology changes looks over the next 25 years and focuses on areas where technology could potentially help to address issues that may arise in the future. So very importantly, framing this, this is future-focused, long-term in outlook to help us plan. Technology's already an important part of our operations.

I think we've spoken in the past about corrections officers already utilizing body-worn cameras. Full body scanners having been introduced at some of our prison sites and what that's then enabled in terms of reducing strip searching, enabling the same activity to be done in a much less intrusive way, both for those in our care as well as the officers who work in that environment. Other technologies, including virtual reality, for training purposes, have been trialed successfully in some sites.

Prisons though, and I think this is the important point to set context, prisons specifically are, by design and necessity, hardened environments. Often built with steel-reinforced concrete, particularly in the high security, maximum security environment. And therefore introducing new technology, particularly on existing assets, can be difficult and expensive, sometimes making it hard to take full advantage of all available technology both now and into the future. Despite that, we are focused on having a plan for how that can be introduced progressively, taking advantage of technology where it

makes sense, but is also affordable. The LTIB looked at three key areas where technology could be used in the future to add value or improve efficiency or safety. Those being in prisons, in the community, and as I've said, in the rehabilitation or integration space. And I thought that would be a helpful focus into those three areas because that's kind of how we've thought about it in the LTIB.

Before I finish, just on the specifics of the mandamus order, just again to help frame what I will be able to talk about rather than what is still subject to proceedings, I will just briefly talk on the mandamus order and the work that is underway as a result. It's important to note that the order arose during existing proceedings, and while it's somewhat independent, I'm going to caveat some of my answers given the wider matter is still sub judice. So I'll just be clear about where those two things sit and where I want to maybe take a step back from the

specifics and give you context rather than answering any specific questions. The order requires that I as chief executive am required to comply with Section 70 of the Corrections Act, in the administration of conditions, specifically of detention in units 12 and 13 at Auckland Prison. That's the maximum security environment. And the order arose during proceedings focused on two units at Auckland Prison related to Section 70 of the act, which provides the detail of the minimum entitlements for exercise.

In short, the judgment touched on several issues, including not consistently meeting open-air requirement, often due to infrastructure constraints, the conflation of exercise and time out of cell, and that inadequate resourcing was not a lawful basis for denying minimum entitlements. We have responded to this. So it was important that we heard, understood, and then took action around the issuing of that mandamus order.

Responding by increasing resourcing, looking at new approaches to unlock regimes to meet the requirements of the order, and I can confirm that the circumstances that gave rise to the order have not occurred since those changes have been made. So I'll give that as the frame to hopefully then enable as much of a constructive conversation as possible whilst not traveling into the license.

Is it subject to appeal? Is that what he's- That's what I was wondering. No. Oh, sorry. It is, and we are not appealing that. So are you finished then? Yes, I am. Right. I'm going to go to Timothy Paul- Can you clarify that?... first. Yeah. To ask him questions. Yeah. Can we just clarify that? Because before, we did a bit of research about whether this was still ongoing, and our understanding was it was Corrections, was it applied for an appeal, and that was denied by the judge, and that now that it just is settled. Right?

We're not appealing. The mandamus order- Yeah... stands, but it is still part of a wider, I think the term is interlocutory, set of matters that is still a live case in the High Court. So that's the interrelationship between the two. There's an order. That is something that we had to apply and comply with from the end of last year, and I can talk to that.

Mm. But I'm not going to talk about any of the wider implications of the case because that would be sub judice. Yeah. Okay. So you go. Yeah. So, over the last two yearsThis committee in interview briefings and during scrutiny week have asked yourself and your leadership whether Corrections is meeting minimum entitlements. And from my recollection, it was always Lee Marshall would answer a lot of the minimum entitlement questions.

Every time, Corrections said to us that, "You guys were meeting the minimum entitlements." So it was a bit of a shock to see the High Court order last year. So what genuine assurances can Corrections give to our committee that this is not just going to be a slap on the wrist for Auckland Prison?

What are you doing to oversee the fulfillment of those entitlements in every single prison? And what will be the consequences for the operation of Auckland Prison not meeting its statutory obligations? Correct. Okay. Well, I'll, I think, take a step back in terms of the specifics of the mandamus order, which was related to units 12 and 13 of Auckland Prison rather than any sort of wider findings. The context of those two units, as you'll be aware, I think you visited that environment, is that they are managing the most complex, violent

people in our system. And within that context, we have put in place regimes that try and prioritize what is in our guiding principles of the Corrections Act under Section 6, maintaining public safety as our primary and paramount consideration in decision-making. What that meant for us on a day-to-day operational basis prior to the mandamus order was that we were very much wanting to and attempting to achieve minimum entitlements, particularly the one-hour access to fresh air every day.

And what we were discovering every day was that the nature of the volatility of the individuals and the care required to ensure we were not inappropriately mixing prisoners in their open-air space to avoid serious assaults and harm of prisoner-on-prisoner, as much as the potential for serious assaults to our officers, that we were seeing incidents that then enabled us to apply an exception to achieving minimum entitlements under 69 [2] B and C, which are enabling us, for reasons of security of the prison operations being threatened or health and safety

of any person in the prison being threatened, lawful reasons for why we would, in those situations, not apply minimum entitlements. Our step back, working through this case and responding to the order, has been for us to confront what we, I think, had drifted into, given the complexity of the people and our desire to achieve that paramount consideration of public safety, we had probably normalized as core regime routine in those two units, exceptions almost every day as a result of concerns of threats to safety and health and safety.

What now, having engaged with the order and having reflected on the judge's clear consideration and therefore issuing of something that is obviously a very substantive position, is that we needed to take a step back and say, "Well, what do we need to do to ensure that we can still account for the safety of our staff, who do incredibly difficult work every day with those most violent people in our system, but still accounts for that as the norm?" Because that is what that unit is achieving. And we need to address the resourcing of people to enable us

to operate a regime that normally can achieve those minimum entitlements. So that was the big change for us in mindset, that we weren't operating on a default of, well, this is just the resourcing that we've applied historically to running a maximum security environment. We now need to think, well, what additional resources do we need? So what I can confirm as a result of that ruling, that order, we've invested $2 million per year in additional frontline staff capability specifically to those units. That has increased the amount of officers who are

available, increased the number of movements that can occur, has anticipated the complexity of the interrelationships, and has made a safe working operation that now properly sees exception rather than default being threat to prisoner safety or health and safety concerns every day. So I can confirm that we are now recording every day with absolute consistency the adherence to and achievement of minimum entitlements.

And where that is not achieved, and I think it's important to note, because some days it won't be, we have got really clear documentation that 69 [2] B or C, one or other, has applied but then justifies why, and we're explicit that that is not as a result of us not adapting and adjusting our staffing and operating regime.

And I think to your broad question of how can we be assured that doesn't apply elsewhere, this particular part of our network and our system is that if you can imagine a triangle of highest risk, most complex right at the top, this is right at the top of that organizational construct. And therefore, I am assured, because we now have that same record of minimum entitlement achievement across our network, that this was the exception. This was the area where we had prioritized as paramount requirement public safety, and that had allowed us to drift into not saying,

actually, things have changed. The complexity and nature of the prisoners has become more unpredictable, more violent, and we now need probably to consider different resourcing to one where the operations weren't kind of putting their hand up. We unfortunately needed this orderTo allow us to properly go in and say, "Something's got to change.

We need to adopt a regime that allows us more normally to maintain those minimum entitlements." You got another question? Well, I was keen to kind of- Duncan Webb. I'll just suck on that. Yeah. Just that last comment just made me really raise an eyebrow, which was, we needed this order. You knew once it was drawn to your attention, and it clearly was, prior to proceedings being filed, you knew you weren't complying with the legal obligations.

Why did it require a judge, a hearing and a judge to say, "Do what the law says," for the corrections to respond in the way it has? If I may, our view of our position was that we were lawfully applying the intent of the act. I referenced the Section 6 principles, the paramount consideration being public safety above all other things, and therefore it did need to be tested.

And so from our perspective- So your view, if I've got the case right, your view was you were having resourcing constraints, which meant you couldn't safely deliver under Section 70, and that was justified. Is that essentially, in a nutshell, your case? That is a simplified version of how we were operating the site. We had considered the risks and the considerations of how we achieved safe operations in that particular unit, those two units.

And our response was to ensure safety was the paramount consideration, and how we then worked our way through that was ensuring that we could confirm that first before anything else, and that was the way in which officers in the line of fire, so to speak, were considering their ability to weigh on a case-by-case, day-by-day basis, whether or not what they were experiencing with a particular prisoner being particularly aggressive or particularly violent on that day amounted to a justification for exception two. And that just gave us the opportunity to reflect, having heard from a judge

how they'd interpreted the law. Right. Because there are exceptions in cases of emergency and so on to not comply with- And threats to safety of prisoners or staff- Right... and threats to health and safety of prisoners as well. But what has been proven by your, the case and your actions is that properly resourced, this was a systemic problem of under-resourcing.

Mm. Rather than some ongoing emergency. That's the question. Well, I would be happy to debate that in terms of, until we got that crystallized for us, our proposition was we were weighing two very complex and careful things about what is the bar, what are the standards of 69 (2) B and C in every single circumstance for every single prisoner in that most complex space, and whether or not, and I think this is what I've tried to convey, whether or not in each circumstance we had drifted into a mode where we were almost assuming, because of the people who we were managing there,

that every day we were going to have a set of circumstances where there would be exceptions that would mean minimum entitlements wouldn't be met. From what I heard you say before, my reading of what you said before, and it's interesting, the interpretation, is that the way you were operating, you had interpreted the law to be such that you could operate the way you were operating.

Correct. Right. Correct. That's exactly the point. And so up to the point of the time that the judge came out with his/her opinion- Yep it was at that point. Because that's what I thought you were trying to say before. It's exactly what I was trying to say. Thank you. But now what you've said is now that you recognize, you obviously recognize the decision, you're spending 2 million a year to try and rectify.

Correct. Yeah. And I might- I might make my question wrap into the long term, if that's helpful. Yeah, please. Because I appreciate the answer, but this is not the first time that Corrections has been challenged over the provision of minimum entitlements, because during COVID, obviously, there was a crisis that justified the reduction of meeting those targets.

Yep. But when we moved out of lockdown and when it was safe to go back to normal, Corrections were not, I think it was at Arohata Women's Prison, were not meeting minimum entitlements. So this is not the first time that this has come up, which is what makes me wonder whether this is a system-wide issue. And from my observations, I wonder whether- Well, next question... this is going to the long-term bit. Hang on, just please let me finish.

It seems to me that a lot of the resourcing is going towards increasing capacity of our prisons and managing the increase in prison population, which has increased by nearly 1,000 people during your tenure as Chief Executive of Corrections, as opposed to the quality of providing the basic minimum entitles entitled to every prisoner, regardless of the severeness or seriousness of their offending.

Yep. What you are describing is the period during and then at the end of COVID, where I think I have come to this committee in its various forms and acknowledged and talked about the weighing of public safety being paramount, our need to ensure that we had sufficient staff who were able to then undertake safe work as we were transitioning and experiencing high turnover, the need to recruit as demand increased, and how we managed those tensions across our network.

So what I would acknowledge is as a result of context changing and our need to be able to account for ourselves absolutely explicitly that we were achieving minimum entitlements, which historically were just that we are. For the previous period from 1995 until 2022/23, there was no explicit documented position that was assuring everyone that every single person was achieving every single minimum entitlement in every single day, but for the exceptions. We are now in a position whereCOVID and the period out required us to be able to evidence that we were achieving those things.

And what we are now, this is where the technology kind of link comes in, we are now in a mode where we have the opportunity, starting in unit 12 and 13 at Auckland Prison, to start to remove the need for officers to be tied up using their own documented approach to recording all of these things, and try and prioritize their time being connected to those in our care, rather than documenting those things.

So we are not in a place where it's a live thing yet. But what we have seen in this work is humans undertaking lots of documented record keeping is not the most effective and efficient, beneficial use of their time. Where technology can play a part, potentially, is the use of CCTV camera footage, the connection of that back into our systems, and that to become more of an automated approach to that absolute, credible, auditable, documented record, freeing up officer time. So that's for us what I think extending our existing technology to something that will help us in the

future reduce the administrative burden on our officers, free them up to spend more time, and create an even stronger evidence trail than what we have today. Because it's a human-based system rather than a machine-based system at the moment. Can I just-- I want to go back to the member's original- Yep... question.

Yep. And I think she's, the second time she's asked the question. Yep. What this committee wants to be assured- Yep is as of today- Yep... can you give assurance to the committee that, the minimum standards or whatever in terms of care- Minimum entitlements, yep.

Whatever way you do it, that's for you. But the committee wants to be assured that you are meeting those, and if you're not, we would love to know where you have areas of concern and by when you would expect to address it. I think that was what the member was originally asking.

If I- So I can keep it short... I'm paraphrasing, but. I can keep it short and say, yes, we are achieving those. And our assurance process for that is, we now have- The data... measures for every single minimum entitlement, for every single prisoner at every single site, and that is being reported on a daily basis.

So what all I'm reflecting on in terms of then moving into the technology is, that is a resource, administratively burdensome part of an officer's day, which is important that we can do from a accounting for ourselves and demonstrating adherence. But technology in the future has probably got a really important part to play in being able to automate that part of what our responsibilities are, to then free back up officer time, which I think is far more appropriately spent engaging with those in our care, trying to get them on a more positive

pathway so they leave us better with brighter prospects. So that's the link, if you like, between- Right. Are we clear on-... an important conversation and the alternative. Okay, I'm going to go to a new line of questioning now to Mr. Todd Simpson. Well, thank you. We'll kind of pick up because I think obviously you said technology focus in the insights briefing, long-term insights briefing.

Yep. Community prisons, rehabilitation. I think we've covered prisons a bit. But keen to understand a bit more what you're thinking in the rehabilitation space because yeah, kind of what did you uncover in putting together the briefing around how technology could help? Because obviously, yeah, we want to make sure that there is opportunities for people that are in your care to get rehabilitation.

Yeah. So, I think acknowledging in my setup that there are really material barriers to just introducing what we might all view as normal technology into a prison context, the hard infrastructure part. We have significant pilot examples where the use of things like virtual reality, which has been used particularly for the education part of our services, is a really valuable vehicle to drive engagement, to drive connection to something beyond the institutional setting,

and can really give a lift to the educational attainments that would otherwise perhaps not be as beneficial. So I think our reflection is virtual reality is a really valuable tool that's been particularly effective, as say, in education and training, giving people access to some certification, qualification that otherwise might not be as practical within a prison context.

And it's shown real gains in terms of those things. I'll turn to my colleagues who are probably much more knowledgeable on this, but I would say what we have seen in other jurisdictions is a move to then include more than education and training within that virtual reality space.

And that, for me, is quite an interesting area to watch because at a human level, I can perhaps appreciate with what is possible now with virtual reality, the opportunity to move people out of what they would have as their day-to-day world into something very different, which might be super helpful in helping them shift their behaviors and mindset.

So that might be an interesting area to focus on, that Juanita might have a view, and then Tom from a delivery perspective. And then the second bit is secure online learning and the ability to ensure that we are keeping abreast with how training and education is being delivered with those normal polytech type courses. And it's been critical for us to be able to shift our mode to enable more and more access of an online nature rather than what has historically been necessary to be our model, face-to-face deliveryAnd that is becoming less and less the mode for some of that

delivery. But I don't know whether you've got any reflections, Juanita, around virtual reality, broadening those to other services, what you might have as a perspective in terms of how that might work with kind of evidence and practice in some of those services, because there'll be pros and cons, right? Yeah, there will be. And in terms of virtual reality within the offense-focused rehabilitative context, I'm actually not aware of how that is being utilized overseas specifically.

But I do know virtual reality more widely for mental health services for some forms of specific illnesses that people might have, has been trialed from probably the late '90s in New Zealand around how we support people to overcome some of those within kind of real-time or real-looking contexts. So some of that would be particularly useful for us to consider in a prison context, where it's also very challenging for people to be in the real-life context.

And the relevance of that to us, I think, is that helps set people up for mental health barriers that are really challenging them to engage in offense-focused rehab proper. As Jeremy said, in terms of virtual reality for education, we've had some really good trials of this within the Department of Corrections and with some really good outcomes in terms of people being able to achieve some NCEA-level results. Not across the spectrum, or across the suite, or across the country, but certainly something that we are seeing some real green shoots from.

Okay. So I'm going to Dr Tracy McLennan. Thank you, Mr Chair. So I had a couple of questions, and Tom, you're probably best placed to answer, because the long-term briefing is really interesting. No free lunches here, Tom. But the briefing's really interesting, so I'm pleased that you're turning your mind to all of the potentials out there. But it's really important that the plans remain adaptable, as you'll know, because rather than being sort of locking in systems that are quickly going to be outdated.

So I'm assuming that that's forming part of your thinking as well. So that's one question, and also noting that the report talks about how new tech can change how crime is detected and managed. So have you also considered whether that sort of increased use of tech could itself drive changes in prison demand over time?

I don't know if that was sufficiently sort of taken into account in the briefing. Okay, let's do those two. We're running out of time a bit. Yeah. Look, my interactions with my peers and colleagues, not just in New Zealand but in Australia, suggest that not really in reduction of crime. However, there is a view around how we could detect just certain interactions between individuals within the custodial environment, and then what could come about as a result of the data that then falls out of that, that we could then leverage

for prisons operations perspective. Because you can imagine a situation where internal charges, that changes the nature of potentially what you're monitoring, isn't it? Yeah. Yeah, absolutely. Do you just want to touch on, I think the first question was sort of pushing into the how do you maintain progress, long-term view of where you're trying to get to without what you're doing becoming outdated before you've even done it. Did I feel some of that in your question?

Yeah. Because I think that sort of speaks to the three phases- Yeah... of your digital SOI. Yeah. So our focus in our digital statement intent is about strengthening, enhancing, and transforming. And so what we're attempting to do with that is stay connected to, again, my peers, both here and not just in the public sector but the private sector, but also across the ditch in Australia. So on behalf of Corrections, I chair the emerging technologies group of people that sit across the corrections environment, and we meet quite regularly every quarter.

And the things that we talk about and discuss is the emergence of technology and the longevity of that in terms of its use within our environment. So we focus on particular areas. Topics that we've touched on, virtual reality, we have talked about that in the education space. That has limited time. We've talked about AI, it seems the topic du jour of the day, and we know that that in and of itself will have limited time span as well. So we're trying to think beyond that.

What are the next things that come along that we might have to turn our minds to in terms of supporting our particular function in our environment? Okay. And I think about our integration offender management system, which is our core platform where data exists to give us information about every single person in our care, case noting, ability to search, document.

That's a legacy system, an old system which has been maintained over time. We're part of a wider justice system, as you well know. So the interrelationship between a police system, a justice system, and a corrections system that need to seamlessly interact but are at different stages creates a sort of similar tension for us in terms of when do we start thinking about what might be next as a different legacy system, versus what do we need to do today to maintain, stabilize, but sort of improve and enhance the existing system. So I think IOMS is a really good example of how

we get confidence and assurance that we have a stable platform whilst Te Reo Neka is being introduced as the case management system within the courts part of justice, so that we are ready with a stable platform to then start to consider how we might shape our future systems as that starts to come in to perhaps think about, and I think this is a really important principle that we've used as a department. We have to know that we have been really disciplined in the way in which we articulate the operational activities that we want to happen in a particular way, the

workflow-Before we start ambitiously describing what the technology needs to be. Mm. So until we describe the workflow activities that will help that work achieve our outcomes, we're not yet ready for the technology. So we're putting the effort into that- Mm... whilst Te Aureka is coming on, so that we're in a position to potentially invest in some of these technologies.

We're running out of time. We're actually over time. I'm going to have two more questions. Sorry. One more from Tracy, and then I'm going- Very briefly. Yep. So I, and that- Last one... that largely answered it. Cool. So I assume making sure that you're able to measure and monitor- Correct... so that it's in service of rehab and- Correct... to reduce re-offending, not just throughput. Correct. Yep. Correct. Okay. Tama Sopor. Last one.

All right. So long term, looking at the makeup of the prison population- Yep... accepting that you don't make the law, there has been- All the decisions that come into our agency. Yeah. Yep. Yep. Accepting all of that. The proportion of Māori in prison is still worryingly high.

It seems that attempts or efforts to refresh Hōkai Rangi, despite this committee recommending that, have not gone anywhere, and I'd love to hear an update if I'm wrong on that. There was a decision made to remove the Māori Leadership Advisory Board to your leadership, and then to look at the woman's prison population.

That's shot up recently. Under your leadership, there have been the Waikeria riots, the high-profile escape of Phillip Smith, and other really serious incidents, and incidences of escapes resulting in innocent members of the public being harmed.

Okay, this is long-term insights. So long-term insights. So let's- Long-term insight, how are you going to change all of that? Because it's not a great record. Okay. I am- That's not even touching on the internal allegations of bullying and internal cultures that is in the media right now. Yeah. That's lots of questions. My short answer to that is, we work in a very complex part of the wider system of public services. I am incredibly proud of the work that everyone undertakes every day within a corrections context.

We are but one important part of a whole heap of different agencies and parties that together leads to the consequences that you have described. We have been committed to ensuring that we are confronting some of the realities in our part of the system. You mentioned the bullying matters, and I'll reference our approach to speaking up, standing up, sexual harassment being an absolute focus for us, and over 2,300 people within our organization have already been through really extensive training, not because we've told them they must,

but because they have determined that it's the right thing to do as a result of us pushing in and saying, "It's incredibly important for us more than anyone else to lead well and to be very conscious of decency and dignity in all of our work." So I'd just park that particular point in there. Yeah. And I think to your questions around Hōkai Rangi, addressing the overrepresentation of Māori- Mm... and particularly around women, it is a long-term intractable challenge- Sure... that is clearly not a corrections alone issue.

We though have heard and understand our role and responsibility in ensuring that we don't sit on our hands and do nothing. And I think between yourself and other members historically of this committee, we have been challenging ourselves as to whether or not targets or key areas of focus are important to shift us away from not being able to apply clear, operationally relevant change to some of these matters. And so to that end, just last week we had a pretty engaging conversation. By that I mean constructive tension in an executive context to ask ourselves,

were we super clear on where we thought we could have the biggest impact to ensure that we were confident that we were doing all we could, both in our agency as well as across the sector, to understand what is a small part, but an incredibly important part of the growth in the women's prison population. Which is those women who are coming into our part of the system on relatively low-level offenses-... that may have been recidivist in nature.

And so that's an area that you have asked us about, and I think the chair had a particular interest in that when we were last talking about- That's right... this as a sector. Yeah. And we have definitely enrolled our colleagues in Justice to understand the data- Mm... to understand what's happening, and to determine what we can practically do that actually starts to demonstrate that we can make a difference, at least in a small part, rather than just keeping it in frameworks, descriptions, and intent.

So that is my reflection. It's incredibly rewarding work, incredibly difficult work, but I'm 100% proud of what I know to be the commitment that every single member of our organization has in trying to make a difference, and enable people to leave us in a better state with brighter prospects. But it's a tough job, but I'm delighted that we've got incredible people doing that work every day.

Hey, well, thank you for coming, and I can assure you what we will end with in our report is that we look forward to hearing your recommendations- Mm-hmm... your new approach, blah, blah, blah, what you're going to see. Thank you. Mm. But, hey, look, I want to thank you for your role. I know you're moving on, but also to your team. Thank you. We all recognize the exceptionally challenging environment you operate in, but just thank you for coming in today.

Appreciate the time. Talking to you. Thanks. Thank you. Mr. Chair? Yeah. Sorry, could we just... What do you think about just staying in public session and getting on with the hearings and coming back to consideration on this later? I'm just going to look at the officials. Can you come back? Do you mind waiting just while folks-Yeah. Do you mind waiting and then we'll- No, that is done.

They don't need to until afterwards, say? Yeah, we don't have officials on that. Okay. All right. Thank you. Great. So if we just roll on. We're going straight, everyone- Yeah ... we're going straight into a hearing. English language. My list is to,

Piripi Rakau Tribal Authority. If that's you, fantastic. Please come to the table. Looking forward to hearing from you. We're running just a little bit late. We've got five minutes with you. We've read your submission, so if you can just leave us a little bit of time for questioning, that would be fantastic.

Ready when you are. Kia ora tatou. Tuatahi ake, ka mihi ki a koutou, i te komiti whiriwhiri, te komiti whai, ti whakawātea mai i tēnei wā ki a mātou o te Pirirakau. Thank you. My name is Heta Gardiner. I am here on behalf of Te Pirirakau Tribal Authority as our Kaihau Tu.

Te Pirirakau Tribal Authority strongly opposes this bill. It is our view that this bill is a solution looking for a problem. It offers no practical benefit but carries significant constitutional and cultural risk. English is already the dominant language of our courts, our schools, commerce, and in our streets.

Around 95% of people here in New Zealand speak English, compared to 4.28% that speak Māori, and 0.5% of the population reported to be using sign language according to our most recent census data. 95% versus 4.28 versus 0.5. The English language is not at risk.

So what problem are we actually solving? It was the Māori language that was removed from early childhood education material. It is the use of Māori language that has been reduced within government departments and in some cases removed from correspondence with international partners.

The Māori Language Commission has warned the Māori language is at risk. And just this very week, this week, the Māori language voices on our airwaves have been diminished with substantial funding cuts. There is a genuine concern for iwi radio. It is the Māori language that is under attack, and a bill to affirm the status of the already dominant language and now viewed as a waste of time and a waste of resource and a blatant example of political posturing.

Unlike Te Reo Māori and New Zealand Sign Language, English is not vulnerable and does not require legislative protection. Elevating it to official status doesn't improve public services. It simply dilutes the specific protections meant for at-risk languages.

Te Reo Māori is a taonga under Article 2 of Te Tiriti o Waitangi. The Crown has a duty of active protection, not just passive tolerance. When one language is still recovering from state-sponsored suppression, legislating the dominance of the already dominant language is inconsistent with the principles of the Tiriti o Waitangi.

This bill doesn't sit in a vacuum. It's part of a broader shift towards linguistic monoculturalism. It signals to Māori that our language and our place in public administration is becoming at best optional or secondary, and at worst now surplus to requirements.

The bill states that legislative recognition of the status of English as an official language will not affect the status or use of Te Reo Māori and New Zealand Sign Language as the two other official languages in New Zealand will not affect the status or use of Te Reo Māori. That's just not true.

Anti-Reo Māori sentiment has already been emboldened by government legislation. We will have dialogue suggesting we don't need to consider Te Reo Māori because we use English and that is already an official language of New Zealand. That is the type of thinking that will infect our society if this legislation is to pass. And to think that won't be the case is naive.

Canvas the opinions of those individuals that support this bill, and you'll find that very sentiment is prominent in the thinking. So what problem are we actually solving? Pirirakau Tribal Authority steadfastly opposes the English Language Bill. Tēnā tātou.

Thank you very much. I've got Dan Rosewarne, who's going to ask you a question. Yeah. Hey, thanks for that contribution. I was just wondering if there's been any international examples where you've seen where similar legislation has improved public or administration outcomes.

And if you have, what lessons could we potentially take from that? Sorry, could you ask the question again? Yeah. So any examples internationally where a bill like this has actually improved public outcomes. I see you mentioned here about the danger of following the US model. Yes.

Has there been other similar bits of legislation that have led to- It being better for the lives of that nation or? So having a bill like this improving things? Yeah. Have you seen the opposite or? No. Okay. Can I ask you a question? It's a very good submission.

Well, the degradation of Te Reo Maori is something that is maintained in most treaty claims. And so if that is denigrated through legislation, then that is a breach.

Thank you. Kia ora koutou. Right. Graham. Right, yeah, there you are. Cool. Thank you. We've seen you before. Yes. Yes. We've got a few people we've seen before in the ref-- Yes. Yes. Thank you for having me. You have the written submission. If anyone has any questions, interrupt me at any point.

Otherwise, I'm probably telling you things you might already know. The bit that's slightly interesting to me about this is, my view is that English is already an official language. There's probably some legal debates. I would say it's de jure, like as a matter of law, it's an official language. Others, de facto official language. So my suggestion would be careful.

The concept of official language is something we put in a lot of treaties, signing with countries which use other languages. And so if there's any implication in passing this bill that we're making English an official language for the first time, rather than it's been an official language under the common law and we're just moving into statute, like we moved the law of contempt of court into statute, which members of this committee will have sat through.

One thing, moving something that's in the common law, protected by that, and now we're going to protect in the statute. But be careful before saying this is not an official language at the moment and we're making it one, because that might have some effects for things that have happened in the past where some foreigner overseas has had to apply to a New Zealand court in one of the official languages of New Zealand, and they always do that in English.

And if English isn't an official language. And so mostly my submission is, I don't mind where the protection of English as an official language is. It could be in the common law, as I would say it currently is. It could be in statute. Probably not much of a difference. But be careful in how you're doing this. We're very keen on your clause 17, which you haven't mentioned yet. So hopefully Dr. Xu Nan is going to talk about it.

Yeah, I think just to pick up on that particular point, there are other places where English is not an official language, but a de facto language. Yes. Like England. So are you saying that if they kept it as it is, then from an international law or convention perspective, that remains it is. But the minute you make it into an official language, then it has implications to any agreement prior to that.

It could, and the risks are probably not that-- It's not going to happen a lot, but you can imagine someone trying it in a court. And it's just like, this isn't going to happen much. It's the risk. It's that you could add or change a few words in the introduction, depending on how you put this into law if you do. Just to make sure everyone's clear, it's always been an official language of New Zealand or has been since 1840, would be my date. Just be careful because if you say it's now an official language, for the first time,

someone will make the argument. And my guess would be like, if someone was to make the argument now, they'd go to court, and the court would tell them, "Don't be silly. English is an official language. Of course, you apply in a New Zealand court in English." But this says, "Use an official language," and there are only two official languages, and the court would just say, "You're wrong." But if you put something in a statute which, no, no, Parliament's actually very clear.

This is the first time English has ever been an official language.Someone will give it a go, and someone will have to spend $10,000 on a lawyer defending it. I think that was basically it, because I think-- Yeah.

Are you there? Good afternoon. We've got five minutes with you. Kia ora. My name's Remu, and I want to be really clear from the outset that I think this bill has the potential to cause a lot of harm across our society, and I oppose it for a whole bunch of reasons.

I appreciate you've had a really busy day already, so please bear with me. I'll take my written submission as read. I did answer the three questions that the committee asked of submitters. So, I'll just quickly go over with you why I believe that framing it as a common sense tidy-up or administrative tidy-up to reflect a so-called linguistic reality of our country is deeply concerning to me. I believe the harm that this bill will cause will be measurable and long-lasting.

Some may say it's only symbolic, but symbolism shapes norms, which shapes behavior, which shapes outcomes. And a law that singles out English like this is meant to send a hierarchy symbol that English is the real language of the country, that it is the priority, and that the other official languages are optional extras. And this matters because as we all know, te reo Maori and New Zealand Sign Language were not made official languages through tidy-up processes.

They were fought for. And that was because the users of those languages faced suppression, exclusion, and many, many barriers to participation in everyday life in our society. And that's mostly, by and large, what official language status is for. It's to protect, to resource, and to normalize languages that have been marginalized.

I have got my notes up, and I've got my submission up, and I will leave a fair bit of time for you to ask questions. But I just did want to also go over the three reasons why a government would undertake language planning. And this is language planning, despite it being a very poor attempt at it. So the three main reasons would be linguistic, where a government has specific aims around protecting a language. And as far as I understand it, this has not been given as one of the specific aims of this bill.

And across the world, you don't get this with English because it is a dominant language. It's a world language. The second reason why a government undertakes language planning is semi-linguistic, where they're attempting to make societal functions easier. So for example, Germany reviewed its spelling system, and the government made official changes for the sake of convenience.

China introduced a specific pinyin in order to standardize things in communication. And the final reason why a government might undertake language planning is extra linguistic. So this is where governments are giving specific languages, specific status, and role in law and policy.

And this is the most politically charged and motivated, where governments are directly intervening in what goes on around language use in a country. And this can look like the planned introduction of a mother tongue, as what happened in post-independence Malaysia, but it can also look like designating what official language use is in a country.

Making English an official language is allocating legal recognition and institutional roles to a languageOur country is highly multilingual, home to about 160 languages. So in countries like ours, language planning is mostly used to address the status and the resourcing of minoritised, marginalized, and indigenous languages, because by its nature, the dominant language does not require such designation in law. As mentioned by other speakers, it is also already de facto official.

So this bill, I believe, primarily functions as a political signal about national identity and creating a hierarchy of languages, which will have real effects. And I want to mihi to some of the speakers before me who have outlined the specific effects it will have on te reo Māori and tangata whenua, and I would also refer members of the Select Committee back to Waitangi Tribunal claim Wai 11.

And please just go over and read that report. It adds a lot of context to why designating English an official language attempts to put it on the same level as te reo Māori and New Zealand Sign Language, and that is simply not the case. I'll leave you a bit of time to ask me any questions.

We're just about out of time now, but I'm going to allow one question from Dr. Sunan. Thank you. Thank you for that submission, Rimu. What would this bill, in terms of language ideology, mean in the context of Aotearoa's history and the role of either tikanga Māori or te reo Māori? Yeah. In the context of our history, we have spent a lot of time suppressing te reo Māori and New Zealand Sign Language.

But putting it on a pedestal, putting English on a pedestal like this implies that the use of te reo Māori and NZSL has to be balanced against English, and this really, really risks undermining the language revitalization that has already happened, and continues to happen, and will happen regardless in community. But the point of entrenching English as an official language is ideological.

It is encouraging cultural dynamics by implying that English isn't well-resourced, that it isn't well-supported. It's an entirely unnecessary bill. It's not going to solve any communication problems, because those problems do not exist. It is ludicrous to suggest otherwise.

adds nothing from an instrumental point of view, and I don't really want to be drawn into the political reasons why we may be going through this whole exercise. And I've given you some suggestions- Yes, very helpful... in my submission about how you could do that. That's the proper place, or perhaps in the Bill of Rights Act or somewhere else, but spelling out all the three official languages and ways would be, if we're going to bother with codification, that's a better way forward.

Well, I'm sure that the Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb wants to sort of raise- Yeah... constitutional matters with you. Dr. Professor, very thank you again for coming in. Whereas if someone who is a Te Reo speaker is given a speeding ticket in English They haven't got an argument. So the languages are not of equal status. And I guess my question is, if we start saying these languages, particularly Te Reo and English, which are written languages, are of equal status, where does that leave us in that is there a dominant language by law, or is that part of the customary law, which is kind of just the background noise that we live in?

Well, just to pick up on that point is I'm suggesting that we collect up our official languages, and status is one thing. about expectations about how they are to be operated. I'm not suggesting from a codification point of view we should change that. We should accept what we've got now and spell that out. Thank you. So, Jetty de Jong. Is she on the line? On the TV. Jetty. She's coming through now. Yeah.

Hello. You're having a busy day. Kia ora. I am, yes. Right. Probably not as busy as you, but anyway All right. You've got five minutes. Sorry. Yeah. All right. Okay. Well, I have asked myself why I felt strongly enough to make a submission on this bill because, as has been pointed out many times, English is already the dominant language spoken here, and it's really just confirming the status quo. But I feel that this bill is actually very damaging to our national unity and also to our Tiriti relationships.

My parents were Dutch immigrants, and my mother often used to say, before she came to New Zealand, there were two things she knew about this country. One was that it was the first country in the world to give women the vote, and the second was that it was an English-speaking country. So they were in no doubt about that in 1951 and 1961, and nothing has changed since.

And I just don't see any reason to formalize the position that we already have. As my husband said in his submission, it's a little bit like proposing that the sparrow should be made an official bird because there are far more of them here than kiwis. I just feel like there's only negatives and disadvantages in progressing this bill, and my biggest concern is that it signals a real lack of respect from the government towards our Tiriti partner.

The official status accorded to Te Reo Maori 50 years ago was only achieved after over a century of struggle to prevent its eradication and a huge effort on the part of Maori leaders at the time. And given that English is not endangered in Aotearoa, New Zealand, or anywhere else in the world, I just feel it's insulting to accord English the same status as our endangered Te Reo Maori, and it signals, in the minds of myself and many people who have, I guess, similar views to me, are some sort of demotion of Te Reo Maori.

English is the language of the colonizers, and it was a tool used by colonizers to suppress the indigenous language. So elevating English to the status of an official language signals to many that, and especially to Maori, a return to those colonial priorities and just a lack of good faith in our Tiriti partnership. And I guess it really just makes me ashamed to be a New Zealander. There are so many legislative attacks already onto Tiriti and Maori in the last few years. This is just another one.

And I want to live in a country that has a good international reputation as a country that honors its promises and all of its people, and this bill is just another example why I can't do that. And as I'm sitting here today, I'm a mother, I'm an education professional, and I'm a board member of a local school. And I was really thinking about what drove me to say, "Yes", and I'll give an oral submission, and it's because I want to represent the next generation of Kiwis. I want their voices to be heard here today, because whatever decisions you make will impact them tomorrow.

So, that's where I'm coming from. But also, as I'm sitting in this room, listening to these colleagues, esteemed colleagues from universities and linguists, I'm thinking, isn't that poetic justice? Because I was born in Ukraine, and knowing the history of English language, it originates from Proto-Indo-European, emerging 6,500 years ago. And here I am today defending, while it's a language that has evolved because of migration, because of cultural exchange, adaptation and borrowing. And here we are, the poetic justice that we have to say, "No, we don't want this

to be an official language." And I do not support this bill. Because ultimately, English is in its ascendancy. It is growing populous. It has spread through exploitation, trade, and colonization. It incorporated words from many cultures and adopted to new environments, was carried and reshaped by diverse people.

So English is fundamentally a language of movement, exchange, and integration. However, there's another point I want to pick up on. And that's what the last speaker was starting to say. As I was writing my submission, it became quite clear that the proposal to make English an official language must also be considered in a broader environment. Many voters, including myself, are not simply evaluating your proposals, but we're also thinking about why this issue?

Why now? Especially with everything that's going on in the world. At what cost to more pressing matters? At what cost to my children? What are the drivers behind prioritizing symbolic legislation such as this? And these concerns, we know that they consume parliamentary time, attention, they divert focus away from substantive policy challenges and contribute to public cynicism about political motive.

So the implications are quite clear. Given its history, it's difficult to argue that English requires protection through official status. Its strength lies in adaptability and global reach, not in legislative reinforcement. At the same time, elevating English formally risks undermining efforts to revitalize Te Reo Maori, creates tension with commitments under Te Tiriti o Waitangi, and reinforces perceptions of exclusion.

Again, many voters like myself are increasingly aware that our parliamentary process has been captured to delay, distract, and derail meaningful progress for our children. So here we are. Biculturalism and multiculturalism is what needs to be protected.

In this context, where public confidence and political priorities is already strained, advancing symbolic legislation of this kind risks reinforcing the view that parliamentary process like this is not aligned with the needs of the people it serves to represent.
